generated: Composite scores recomputed across 10 base methods after the backend expansion: the original 7 statistical/stylistic measures + thematic-allusion density + catalogue density + MEDIUM+ candidate-echo density, all normalized per million words. See data/composite.json for the full matrix.

founders.name: John Adams
founders.tagline: The most Shakespearean Founder by every measure. 61 verbatim quotations and 48 by-name references across his life, 18 thematic Roman character invocations, more than twice the next Founder per million words.

founders.name: Benjamin Franklin
founders.tagline: Shakespeare absorbed as habit, not citation. Almost no quotation, but the highest archaic-form density of any Founder and per-million Shakespeare-overlap second only to Adams.

founders.name: Thomas Jefferson
founders.tagline: The gentleman-reader's Shakespeare. 26 by-name references, zero direct quotations, four thematic Brutus and Caesar invocations across the Federal period.

founders.name: George Washington
founders.tagline: Less literary than the others. The 1778 'band of brothers' passes through his pen at Valley Forge and elsewhere; otherwise the Shakespeare is absent.

founders.name: Alexander Hamilton
founders.tagline: Bottom of the rankings on every literary measure. The one exception is Roman: a 1779 letter calling General Lee 'a little spice of Julius Caesar or Cromwell', plus a reported dinner-party line naming Caesar the greatest man who ever lived.

founders.name: James Madison
founders.tagline: The most thorough silence in the corpus. One medium-confidence direct quotation and one by-name reference across 3.4 million words, with no thematic allusions in the data at all.
